title: Lesson 194
transcript: Okay, in this module, we're going to expand the role of a policy. We've already talked about how a policy provides the foundation necessary for effective security to exist in any organization. And that's true of asset security, specifically a data classification policy. So typically in most organizations you will have a asset classification policy that talks about the importance of protecting assets and the value that they represent to the organization and that of course includes The accountabilities, the responsibilities that go along with that. The owner will always be accountable, not only for protecting their assets, but also in owning, for example, the asset classification policy that really dictates how we protect assets within the organization itself. Now, when you look at any policy, including a data classification policy, you know that a policy is simply a corporate law that communicates management's intentions in how it applies to the organization. In other words, it tells us what is what is not acceptable. and who is accountable and responsible for protecting assets. The owners are always going to be accountable. Everyone else, basically custodians, will be responsible. But a policy communicates what's important. The way that we implement the policy is through the elements that obviously are below that, that we've talked about certainly in domain number one. But obviously the way that we implement policies are through standards, procedures, baselines and also guidelines and a combination of all of those. And as you'll remember standards are specific hardware and software products typically and solutions that can again implement the policy itself. Procedures are step-by-step requirements that need to be followed. And baselines are typically configurations, parameters, for example, that we apply in different technologies that are also mandatory. And guidelines, as the word implies, simply means suggestions. You should or you should not. And when you put all of these elements together, standards, procedures, baselines and guidelines. They are really the security controls that we apply in this case in protecting, for example, data and allows the organization to achieve the requirements of the data classification policy that owners are ultimately responsible for. And where security comes in is obviously in supporting not only the policy itself so that everyone is aware of it, but also in how we use the standards, the procedures, the baselines and the guidelines and how everybody should be educated and being able to use the right products and so on. Now in any policy, we need to obviously understand the requirements of what that policy addresses, which is obviously the value of the assets themselves, which really dictate how we protect that important asset in the end. And so the owners are always accountable. for the contents of the policy itself. And so what adds value to the asset and how we therefore protect it is dictated by a number of elements that you see here up on your screen. And that includes things like laws and regulations because that's what dictates how a company would achieve its compliance. So that's obviously something that would add value to the asset and would require us to protect it to that level. Does the asset have anything to do with privacy requirements which would also add value? Is there a cost of creation for example or using the asset that also adds value? So what you're seeing here on your screen are some of the elements that owners are in the best position to be able to understand because these are all elements that add value to the asset which obviously dictates how we ultimately protect it. The owner is always in the best position to understand all of those factors, those elements such as the ones that you see here that add value and therefore dictate how the asset needs to be protected as it goes through its life cycle. And so what you see here on the next slide is typically what we refer to as the asset life cycle, even though here it says information life cycle. We're using information as an example of an asset that companies would want to protect. But again, information is only one example of a valuable asset, but all assets will go through a life cycle. from the time that we create it or acquire it to the time that at some point in the future we're going to destroy or decommission that asset. the idea here is if we keep track of the asset life cycle it may actually increase or decrease in value and so your asset classification systems that are there to protect your assets need to be able to support an increase in classification or a decrease in classification. And again, it's the owner that is in the best position to be able to understand when something increases in value or decreases in value as it goes through its life cycle but your asset classification system needs to be able to support that so that again what we do is we if the asset increases in value it means that we need more protection mechanisms we need more security to be able to be applied to that asset. If it decreases in value then we have to drop its classification level to something lower which means that again lower security controls will be required in order to protect at always the value of the asset. So the whole bottom line here is that when you implement an asset classification system that supports protecting assets we need to be able to support an increase in classification or a decrease in classification as that asset goes through its life cycle represented here. on your slide as you see and it doesn't matter whether that asset lasts for just a few months or you may have some really long retention periods. The idea is that whatever level it's at that is representing the value is how we protect it. And baselines will dictate what those minimum levels of security are that we need to apply to assets as they go through their asset life cycle, which again could be long. in some cases related to different companies that operate in different industries and may have different compliance requirements, for example.
